topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the faded brochure, the glossy paper crinkling under her thumb. "Seriously? *Camp Whispering Pines*? That place was a complete waste of time." She flipped the page, her expression tightening as she saw the identical logo from the summer of 2018. Across the desk, Marcus leaned back, a slow, almost amused smile playing on his lips. "Well, look what the tide dragged in. You seem particularly unimpressed." Elara didn't look up immediately, instead tracing the outline of a poorly drawn canoe on the pamphlet. "It’s just... a coincidence. Hardly noteworthy." A sharp, almost brittle edge entered her voice as she finally met his gaze, her eyes lingering on his for a fraction of a second too long before snapping back to the paper.

[story 2]

"Are you actually going to tell me you attended Whispering Pines?" Liam asked, his tone laced with a surprising lack of mockery. He crossed his arms, his posture rigid, a slight frown creasing his brow. I felt a familiar tightening in my chest, a subtle recoil. I picked at a loose thread on my sleeve, avoiding his direct line of sight. "Why would I go?" I managed, my voice sounding flat, devoid of any real enthusiasm. "It was an obligation. Nothing special." A muscle twitched in my jaw. I shifted in my seat, the movement stiff, as if trying to physically distance myself from the shared memory. The air around us seemed to thicken with unspoken judgment.

[story 3]

Maya let out a short, humorless puff of air. She tapped the photograph on the screen, a quick, dismissive flick of her finger. "Imagine. Both of us at that ridiculously rustic retreat. It’s almost insulting." Her gaze swept over Alex, cataloging his relaxed posture and easygoing demeanor with visible disapproval. Alex chuckled, a low, warm sound that didn't quite reach his eyes. "So, you’re implying something, Maya?" he inquired, his voice even, challenging. She pushed the screen away, the movement abrupt. "No. I’m just observing the sheer redundancy of it all. Some things are simply beneath notice." She picked up her coffee mug, her grip firm, as if anchoring herself against the sudden, unwelcome familiarity of the shared past.
